title: RBAC v2: structured denial reason codes + optional authz debug header
preview: Problem: With RBAC v2 deny-by-default landing across Gateway + Control Plane, Support and Console UX need consistent, structured signals to answer “why was I denied?” without leaking policy internals or creating side-channel disclosure. Today we return a generic 403 from several stacks and each service/gateway path formats errors differently, making troubleshooting slow (and leading to tickets like SUP-48305/INT-19022). This PR introduces a shared set of denial reason codes and a gated, optional debug header mechanism for admin troubleshooting. Summary of changes: (1) Add canonical AuthzDenialReasonCode enum (and mapping helpers) in the shared authz package. (2) Standardize PermissionDenied error payload shape across gateway + control-plane HTTP handlers (no policy details; includes stable reason code + request id). (3) Add opt-in debug response headers behind BOTH a feature flag and an admin-only permission check, so authorized admins can see a high-level explanation in-flight without needing server logs. (4) Add metrics and logs to quantify deny reasons (for rollout dashboards) while keeping user-visible errors minimal. API/behavior details: - Default behavior (no debug): 403 with error code PERMISSION_DENIED and a structured denial_reason_code field (string) plus request_id; message remains generic ("permission denied"). - Debug behavior (admin-only): if request includes header X-Redwood-Debug-Authz: 1 AND caller has permission rbac.debug_authz AND rbac_debug_headers_enabled flag is on, then response includes headers: X-Redwood-Authz-Decision: deny|allow, X-Redwood-Authz-Reason: <reason_code>, X-Redwood-Authz-Required-Permission: <permission>, X-Redwood-Authz-Scope: <scope_type>:<scope_id>. We intentionally do not include the evaluated role names, bindings, group ids, or full policy graph. - For non-admins or when flag disabled: the request proceeds normally but debug headers are not included; we do not return a different error to avoid oracle behavior (we only log internally that a debug header was requested but suppressed). Denial reason codes included (initial set): - MISSING_PERMISSION (subject lacks required permission for action) - SCOPE_MISMATCH (permission exists but not for requested scope) - SUBJECT_UNRESOLVED (no subject / token mapping failed) - POLICY_VERSION_UNAVAILABLE (policy fetch failed / stale) - EVALUATION_TIMEOUT (authz evaluation exceeded budget) - BREAK_GLASS_REQUIRED (endpoint requires break-glass elevation) - ENDPOINT_UNANNOTATED (sensitive endpoint missing permission annotation; treated as deny in v2 mode) - FEATURE_FLAG_DISABLED (v2 enforcement / endpoint guard disabled; used internally for debug + metrics) Notes on info disclosure: Debug headers are only emitted for callers who already have rbac.debug_authz. This is designed for Org Admin/Security Admin flows in Console and for Support during guided troubleshooting with customer administrators. We also redact in Hosted multi-tenant environments by default (flag off), but enable in dedicated/private dogfood and pilot envs. Test coverage: unit tests for reason code mapping and redaction, gateway integration tests for header gating and consistent payload shape, and a control-plane handler test to validate ENDPOINT_UNANNOTATED denies when v2 enforcement is enabled. Follow-ups: Console “Why denied?” UX will consume denial_reason_code + request_id immediately; debug header view will be exposed only in admin troubleshooting pane (separate PR in console repo).

title: Retention: add change history + tenant export endpoint (for compliance report pack)
preview: Context: The Compliance Reporting Pack MVP needs a deterministic, customer-ready artifact that captures (a) the tenant’s effective retention settings across supported data classes and (b) the auditability of retention changes over time. Today we can fetch the current policy, but we don’t retain a first-class change history nor provide a stable export payload suitable for report generation. This PR adds: (1) retention policy change history persisted in the control plane with immutability guarantees, and (2) a new export endpoint that produces a versioned export (JSON) used by the report pack generator and audit-log-exporter attachment bundling. Scope: control plane only; does not change TTL enforcement in data plane stores. What’s included: 1) Change history: new table retention_policy_change_events with append-only rows capturing actor, source, diff summary, previous_policy_hash, new_policy_hash, request_id, and effective_at. History is written on every successful policy create/update/patch as part of the same transaction as the policy write. Includes backfill for existing tenants: we create a single seed event “imported_current_state” with actor=system and previous hash=null. 2) Export endpoint: GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention/export?format=json&include=history_summary&window_days=90. This returns the tenant’s effective retention config (post-validation, post-defaulting, post-tier constraints) plus an optional bounded history summary. The response is versioned via export_schema_version and is intended to be stable for external evidence packages (see ADR-018). 3) Authorization/RBAC: export requires role=SecurityAdmin or ComplianceAuditor (new permission RETENTION_EXPORT_READ). Endpoint is tenant-scoped; cross-tenant access is blocked at the router layer + DB query layer. All exports emit an audit log event retention.exported with tenant_id, actor_id, and export_schema_version (no policy contents logged). 4) Redaction: Export payload intentionally excludes free-form notes fields and internal-only evaluator debug fields. Actor email is replaced with actor_display_name + actor_type + actor_id. 5) Rate limiting + caching: export response supports If-None-Match via an ETag computed from (effective_policy_hash + latest_change_event_id + query params) to reduce repeated generation during report pack assembly. 6) Tests: unit tests for diff summarization and hash stability; integration tests for RBAC and ETag behavior; snapshot test for export v1.0 payload shape. API details: Response (format=json) includes: export_schema_version (e.g., "1.0"), generated_at, tenant_id, tier, region_scope, effective_policy (data_class -> ttl_days, purge_semantics, legal_hold_supported flag), defaults_applied (per class), constraints (min/max per tier), and history_summary (bounded list with event_id, occurred_at, actor_type, action, affected_classes, previous_hash, new_hash). Example snippet: { "export_schema_version": "1.0", "generated_at": "2025-02-14T19:22:10Z", "tenant_id": "tnt_1g8...", "tier": "Dedicated", "effective_policy": { "audit_logs": { "ttl_days": 365, "purge_semantics": "hard_delete" }, "request_response_logs": { "ttl_days": 30, "purge_semantics": "hard_delete" } }, "history_summary": [ { "event_id": 4812, "occurred_at": "2025-02-14T18:01:00Z", "actor_type": "user", "action": "update", "affected_classes": "audit_logs", "previous_hash": "rp_7f9...", "new_hash": "rp_8a1..." } ] } Notes/decisions: - We store both a normalized policy hash and a small diff summary (affected data classes + ttl deltas) so audits can see what changed without reconstructing the full prior policy from logs. - History_summary is bounded to prevent accidental large exports; default window_days=90 with max=365. Full history export is intentionally deferred (non-goal) until we validate customer needs + storage costs. - This is designed to integrate with ENG-18441 (report generator) and audit-log-exporter PR-627 (attachment bundling). Rollout: Behind feature flag retention_exports_v1 for 1 week; enabled for internal tenants + 2 design partners; then GA. DB migration is safe and backward compatible. No changes to existing retention CRUD endpoints besides writing change events. Risks/mitigations: - PII leakage in change events: actor is stored as IDs; display name is resolved at export time via directory service, and can be disabled in Private deployments. - DB growth: events are small; we will apply a long TTL (7y) only for Dedicated/Private tenants by default, else 1y for Hosted (configurable later). - Consistency: policy write and change event write are in one transaction; export reads at REPEATABLE READ to avoid mismatched policy/hash. Screenshots: none (API-only).

title: Bootstrap audit exporter service skeleton + CI + local dev harness
preview:
## Summary
Bootstraps the new `audit-log-exporter` service repository with a minimal runnable skeleton, CI workflows, a base configuration model, and a local dev harness to support early iteration.

This is the first landing step for ENG-5139. No adapters, no delivery semantics, and no production wiring in this PR; those will follow in subsequent PRs.

## Motivation / Context
We need a dedicated service to read audit events (initially via stubbed source in local dev), apply tenant scoping, and deliver to downstream destinations with retries/checkpointing. Before we can iterate on architecture and adapters, we need:
- Standard project layout + build tooling
- Baseline CI (lint/test/build, container build)
- A config model (typed) that can evolve with control-plane config sync
- A local harness so devs can run the service end-to-end without needing the full control plane

## What’s included
- Service scaffold (Go)
  - `cmd/audit-exporter` main entrypoint
  - `internal/server` HTTP server with `/healthz` + `/readyz`
  - `internal/config` base config model + validation
  - `internal/logging` structured logging setup (json)
- CI / tooling
  - GitHub Actions for: `go test`, `go vet`, `golangci-lint`, `gofmt` check, and Docker build
  - Dependabot for Go modules + GitHub Actions
  - Makefile targets: `make test`, `make lint`, `make run`, `make docker-build`
- Local dev harness
  - `docker-compose.yml` to run exporter + a lightweight stub source (placeholder) + optional Prometheus
  - `./scripts/dev/generate_synthetic_events.sh` to emit sample audit events to stdout/file (temporary)
- Base config model (initial)
  - `listen_addr`, `log_level`
  - `tenant_id` (dev-only for now; will be replaced by multi-tenant config sync)
  - placeholder `export` section for future destinations (intentionally not wired)

## Not included (intentionally)
- No real event ingestion from Redwood services yet
- No destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
- No checkpointing / delivery guarantees
- No filtering engine
- No secret management integration

## Testing
- Unit tests for config parsing + validation
- CI builds container image to ensure Dockerfile stays green
- Manual:
  - `make run` (starts HTTP server)
  - `docker compose up --build` (runs exporter + harness components)

## Follow-ups
- ENG-5132: Control plane export config API
- ENG-5144/5151/5156: Destination adapters
- ENG-5168: Metrics + tracing
- ENG-5189: Threat model items (egress allowlist hooks, secrets handling)

## Notes for reviewers
- Config model is deliberately minimal and will churn; goal is to establish patterns (validation, defaults, env overrides).
- Health endpoints are required for upcoming k8s deployment.

Closes: ENG-5139 (bootstrap portion)

title: Add audit log events for residency policy create/update/delete + wire to audit-log-exporter
preview: Context: As part of Hosted data residency enforcement we need auditable change history for residency pinning configuration (org/project/api-key level). This PR emits audit log events on residency policy mutations (create/update/delete) and wires those events into the existing audit-log-exporter pipeline so they are available in Console audit log views and customer exports. Scope: Control plane (residency policy service) emits events; exporter normalizes/ships; no changes to request-time enforcement here. Event types (v1): residency_policy.created, residency_policy.updated, residency_policy.deleted, residency_policy.override_applied (admin-only, gated behind flag). Payload highlights: actor (user_id/service, auth_type, ip where available), target (org_id, project_id nullable, api_key_id nullable), policy_id, allowed_regions, primary_region, allow_cross_region_fallback, effective_from, previous_values (for updates, limited), request_id/trace_id for correlation, and metadata (event_version=1, source=control_plane, environment). Security constraints: do NOT include any customer prompt/content; for api_key identifiers we include api_key_id (internal UUID) and last4 only; we do not emit raw key material. For update events, previous_values are limited to residency-related fields only and exclude any free-form notes/description fields. Exporter notes: events are routed to the same destination as other config/audit events; this is metadata-only and allowed per ADR-021 boundaries. Testing: unit tests for event emission on CRUD, exporter schema validation tests, and an integration test that asserts event appears in audit-log-exporter queue with expected fields. Rollout: guarded by feature flag residency_audit_events_v1 (default off); can be enabled per environment. Follow-ups: Console UI will consume these events in DES-611 work; docs to be updated in redwood-docs PR-1189. Checklist: - [x] Emit events on create/update/delete - [x] Add admin override event (flagged) - [x] Add exporter mapping + schema validation - [x] Add tests - [x] Security review sign-off - [x] SRE review for exporter volume impact

title: Retention policy evaluator + cache (precedence, invalidation, unit tests)
preview:
Summary: Adds a retention policy evaluation module to the control plane with an in-memory cache + explicit invalidation. Includes unit tests that lock down precedence/override rules and edge cases (region overrides, disabled logging modes, legacy TTL fields). This is the shared library used by policy CRUD endpoints, TTL enforcement config resolution, and export/report surfaces.

Motivation: We need a single authoritative place to evaluate effective retention settings per tenant + deployment mode + region. The evaluator also needs to be fast enough for the request path (config lookups and downstream enforcement jobs) without pushing policy logic into each data store.

Key behavior (precedence rules): (1) Explicit tenant policy (most specific scope wins: data_class+region > data_class > global) (2) Tenant logging config TTL fields (legacy) ONLY when no explicit retention policy exists for that data class (to keep backwards compatibility during rollout) (3) Tier defaults (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) as the final fallback. For disabled logging modes (e.g., request logs disabled), evaluator returns `enabled=false` and `ttl_seconds=0` regardless of tier default. Legal hold is not implemented here; the evaluator surfaces a `hold_state=unknown` field to be filled by the hold service in a follow-up (kept out of this PR to avoid coupling).

Caching: Adds `RetentionPolicyCache` keyed by (tenant_id, environment, region, schema_version). Cache is LRU bounded with a short TTL (default 30s) to protect control plane DB during bursts and background enforcement scans. Explicit invalidation happens on successful policy writes (Create/Update/Delete) and on `RetentionPolicyChanged` events emitted by the policy service. Cache entries never mix tenants; keys are fully qualified and values contain only evaluated outcomes (no raw policy JSON) to reduce accidental logging/leakage.

Invalidation semantics: Invalidation is immediate on the writer instance and best-effort broadcast via the existing internal event bus. For instances that miss the event, the cache TTL bounds staleness. Unit tests cover invalidation on version bump + deletion (ensuring it falls back to legacy or tier defaults correctly).

Observability: Emits metrics: `retention_policy_cache_hits_total`, `retention_policy_cache_misses_total`, `retention_policy_cache_evictions_total`, and `retention_policy_eval_latency_ms` (histogram). Labels are constrained to `{result, data_class}`; no tenant_id labels.

Tests: Adds `RetentionPolicyEvaluatorTest` with coverage for: global TTL, data-class override, region override, precedence vs legacy TTL, disabled logging, invalid/missing region, and cache invalidation behavior. Also adds a small concurrency test to ensure no cross-tenant contamination and that parallel evaluation is safe.

Rollout: Evaluator is gated behind `RETENTION_POLICY_EVAL_V1` feature flag in the policy service and referenced by the new CRUD endpoints in PR-28410. This PR intentionally does not change any existing enforcement jobs; it only provides the evaluation module + cache used by enforcement wiring in follow-ups.

Notes: Aligns with ADR-017 decision to evaluate policies in control plane (not data plane), with bounded caching for performance and auditable change semantics.

Observability: Emits metrics: `retention_policy_cache_hits_total`, `retention_policy_cache_misses_total`, `retention_policy_cache_evictions_total`, and `retention_policy_eval_latency_ms` (histogram). Labels are constrained to `{result, data_class}`; no tenant_id labels.

Tests: Adds `RetentionPolicyEvaluatorTest` with coverage for: global TTL, data-class override, region override, precedence vs legacy TTL, disabled logging, invalid/missing region, and cache invalidation behavior. Also adds a small concurrency test to ensure no cross-tenant contamination and that parallel evaluation is safe.

Rollout: Evaluator is gated behind `RETENTION_POLICY_EVAL_V1` feature flag in the policy service and referenced by the new CRUD endpoints in PR-28410. This PR intentionally does not change any existing enforcement jobs; it only provides the evaluation module + cache used by enforcement wiring in follow-ups.

title: Add TTL/retention policy references to tenant logging config model
preview:
Title: Add TTL/retention policy references to tenant logging config model

Context
Retention controls requires a single place to express what data classes are eligible for storage and (when stored) what retention policy applies. Today our per-tenant logging config describes enablement + sampling/fields for request logs / traces / etc, but it has no concept of retention TTL or a link to the retention policy service.

This PR extends the tenant logging config model and API surfaces to include optional TTL/retention references. It also includes a safe, backwards-compatible migration path so older SDKs/clients keep working.

Scope
- Adds fields to TenantLoggingConfig to reference a retention policy and/or per-store TTL overrides.
- Adds storage + migrations (DB columns + JSON blob) and read/write plumbing.
- Adds compatibility behavior so the API continues accepting legacy payloads.
- Adds notes + guardrails for server-side defaults (do not reduce retention silently).

What changed
1) Model/API
- New top-level optional reference:
  - retention_policy_id: string | null (opaque ID issued by retention-policy-service)
  - retention_policy_version: int | null (pinned version at time of config write; optional, for future audit/report exports)
- New optional per-store overrides:
  - retention_overrides: map keyed by data class (request_logs, audit_logs, traces, metrics, artifacts)
  - each override supports ttl_days (int) and enabled (bool) as a bridge for stores that are not yet fully wired to policy evaluation

Notes:
- These fields are additive and do not change behavior in the data plane by themselves.
- TTL enforcement for request/audit logs is wired in follow-up PRs; this PR focuses on config + persistence.

2) Persistence
- DB migration adds nullable columns on tenant_logging_config:
  - retention_policy_id (text)
  - retention_policy_version (int)
  - retention_overrides (jsonb)
- For tenants with existing configs, columns default to NULL (meaning "use platform defaults" / "no explicit retention policy configured").

3) Backwards compatibility
- API continues to accept legacy payloads without retention fields.
- API will return the new fields as null/empty when not set; older clients ignoring unknown fields remain unaffected.
- We explicitly avoid auto-populating retention_policy_id during migration to prevent accidental retention changes without an explicit customer/admin action.

4) Validation
- Basic validation added at the config boundary (not full retention-policy-service validation):
  - ttl_days must be within [1, 3650] if provided (hard safety bound; tier-specific constraints enforced in retention-policy-service)
  - only known data classes accepted for retention_overrides keys
  - cannot set retention_overrides for a data class that is disabled in logging config unless enabled=true is also set in the same update (prevents confusing partial configs)

5) Docs / dev notes
- Adds internal developer note in /docs/internal/retention/ explaining intended precedence:
  - per-store override (if present) > retention_policy_id (if wired for that store) > platform defaults
  - for stores not yet wired to policy evaluation, override acts as the single source of truth

Illustrative API payload
PATCH /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/logging-config
{
  "request_logs": { "enabled": true, "sampling": 1.0 },
  "traces": { "enabled": false },
  "retention_policy_id": "rp_01HRK4...",
  "retention_overrides": {
    "request_logs": { "ttl_days": 30 },
    "traces": { "ttl_days": 7, "enabled": false }
  }
}

Migration plan
- Online migration (expand/contract):
  1) Expand: add columns + write path that can populate them.
  2) Deploy app.
  3) Optional backfill is NOT included (intentional) because defaults are tier-based and owned by retention policy evaluation.
  4) Contract (future): once all clients and internal tooling are updated, we can remove legacy-only code paths.

Risk/impact
- Low runtime risk; config reads/writes add a small JSONB payload.
- No retention behavior changes without follow-up wiring in purge jobs / policy evaluator.
- Guardrail: we do not set TTL defaults in the DB migration to avoid accidentally shortening retention.

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - JSON serialization/deserialization of retention fields
  - validation for unknown keys / invalid ttl_days
  - merge semantics when patching (existing retention_overrides preserved unless explicitly updated)
- Migration verified on a staging snapshot (read old configs; write new; read back)

Rollout
- Behind feature flag for UI exposure; API supports fields unconditionally.
- Console work will land separately (DES-912).

Related
- Linear: ENG-18420 (Retention policy service API + storage)
- Linear: PM-842 (Retention controls + compliance reporting pack)
- Follow-ups: request logs TTL enforcement, audit logs TTL enforcement

---
Commits (squash-merged)
- model: add retention fields to TenantLoggingConfig
- api: accept/emit retention_policy_id + retention_overrides
- db: add retention columns + migration
- validation: ttl bounds + known data classes
- tests: config patch + serde cases

Files/areas changed
- services/control-plane/tenants/logging_config_model.ts
- services/control-plane/tenants/logging_config_handlers.ts
- services/control-plane/db/migrations/2025_02_11_add_retention_fields_to_logging_config.sql
- libs/config-schema/tenant_logging_config.json
- docs/internal/retention/logging-config-retention-fields.md

CI
- build-and-test (linux/amd64): PASS
- lint: PASS
- db-migration-check: PASS
- api-contract-tests: PASS

Review conversation
- Ethan Park: "Can we clarify precedence between per-store override and retention policy ID? Also please confirm no defaults are applied during migration." Benji Okafor: "Added precedence note + explicitly documented no backfill/no default TTL in migration."
- Marcus Lin: "Please ensure we don’t accidentally allow setting retention overrides for audit logs before audit log retention is wired; could create expectation." Benji Okafor: "Kept it allowed but marked as config-only; added dev note + TODO tags for wiring. Also validation ensures it doesn’t enable a disabled store silently."
- Allison Grant: "Any concerns with JSONB size / query patterns?" Benji Okafor: "Overrides are small; reads are per-tenant; no indexed queries needed. Added limit on number of override keys and max payload size." 
- Chloe Martin: "Console will need change history / diffing; can we keep the shape stable?" Benji Okafor: "Kept schema minimal and versioned; added retention_policy_version placeholder for future export."

Outcome
Merged (squash) into main. No immediate customer-visible change until the policy evaluation + purge jobs are enabled per data store.

title: Add retention policy CRUD endpoints + validation + audit trail
preview:
Summary: This PR adds the first cut of retention policy management to the control plane: CRUD endpoints, request/response types, validation rules, and audit log events for all policy mutations. This unblocks wiring retention enforcement in downstream stores and enables the compliance reporting pack to export tenant retention configuration.

Context: As part of the Retention controls + compliance reporting pack project (ENG-18420 / PM-842), we need a durable, auditable policy object that can be referenced by logging/telemetry subsystems. Policy evaluation and caching will be layered in a follow-up PR (see pr-28427) but the API surface and auditability need to land first so dependent teams can integrate.

What’s included: (1) RetentionPolicy model + persistence in control-plane DB (tenant-scoped, immutable id, mutable spec). (2) REST endpoints: POST /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policies, GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policies/{policy_id}, PATCH /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policies/{policy_id}, DELETE /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policies/{policy_id}, GET /v1/tenants/{tenant_id}/retention-policies (with basic filtering/pagination). (3) Validation: name length/charset, description length, per-data-class TTL bounds (coarse bounds for v1; detailed tier-aware bounds and disabled-logging interactions will be tightened in pr-28455 + schema doc update), consistency checks (e.g., legal_hold cannot be set via this API), and rejection of unknown data classes. (4) Audit trail: emits audit events for create/update/delete with actor, tenant, policy_id, diff summary, and request correlation id; ensures PATCH includes an explicit field mask so the audit diff is stable. (5) RBAC: endpoints require SECURITY_ADMIN or PLATFORM_ADMIN for tenant; read/list also permitted for AUDITOR role (tenant-scoped) to support evidence workflows.

Not included / follow-ups: (a) Policy evaluation and precedence rules (pr-28427). (b) Attaching policies to tenant logging config and migration/back-compat (pr-28455). (c) Console UI surfaces (DES-912). (d) Export endpoint and change history (pr-28526).

API shape notes: Spec is modeled as a map keyed by data_class (request_logs, audit_logs, traces, metrics, artifacts) with {ttl_days, purge_mode}. For now purge_mode is accepted but only 'hard_delete' is supported; values are validated and stored so we don’t break clients as we add modes. PATCH supports partial updates for name/description/spec; updates are last-write-wins but include an optimistic concurrency token (etag) returned on GET; clients may send If-Match to avoid overwrites.

Security/Compliance: All mutation endpoints log an audit event; we intentionally do not allow setting legal hold via this API (hold is a separate workflow with approvals). Payloads are validated to avoid accidentally allowing unbounded retention; server enforces max TTL bounds even if client is compromised.

Testing: Added unit tests for request validation, RBAC gates, etag behavior, and audit event emission. Added integration-ish tests using the in-memory DB harness for CRUD lifecycle and pagination. Manually tested using curl against dev control plane (notes in commit message).

Rollout: Safe to merge behind feature flag retention_policy_service.enabled (default off in production). The API routes are registered only when flag is on; migrations are included but do not affect existing tables. We will enable flag in staging once pr-28427 is merged so evaluators can start consuming these objects.

Screenshots/Docs: None (API-only). Docs update tracked in ENG-18460.

Open questions: (1) Tier-aware TTL min/max bounds: in this PR we use conservative global bounds; Product/Security to confirm exact defaults per Hosted/Dedicated/Private in the schema doc. (2) Data class naming: we aligned on audit_logs vs request_logs based on Security thread; confirm final canonical list before public docs.

title: Add Datadog Logs destination adapter + audit tag/facet mapping
preview: Summary: This PR adds a Datadog Logs destination adapter to the audit-log-exporter and establishes a stable mapping from audit event schema v2 -> Datadog tags/facets. It also introduces rate-limit aware batching behavior (primarily for 429/503) so we degrade gracefully under Datadog intake constraints while preserving at-least-once semantics. Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we support Splunk HEC, S3, and Datadog Logs. Datadog differs from Splunk in that (a) intake is strongly rate-limited, and (b) the best user experience is via consistent tagging/faceting for filtering/dashboards in Datadog. This PR aligns with ENG-5156 and the delivery semantics ADR (at-least-once, downstream dedup via event_id). Design notes (high-level): Adapter: Adds `datadog_logs` destination type with `site`, `intake_path`, and `api_key_secret_ref`. We send batches to `https://http-intake.logs.{site}/api/v2/logs` (default) with JSON payloads. Each audit event becomes one log item with a message, attributes, and tags. Tag/facet mapping: We map audit event fields into a consistent set of Datadog tags for filtering. Primary tags: `redwood.tenant_id`, `redwood.workspace_id`, `redwood.category`, `redwood.action`, `redwood.resource_type`, `redwood.actor_type`, and optionally `redwood.actor_id` (only when allowed by audit policy). We also set `service=redwood-audit-export`, `ddsource=redwood`, and `env=<region|deployment>` (exporter runtime env). We keep the raw payload minimal: we do not include request bodies, prompt text, or free-form metadata; event context is limited to schema v2 allowed fields and pre-existing redactions. Rate-limit aware batching: The adapter maintains a per-destination adaptive batch size. On 429, we back off with jitter and reduce batch size (floor 1). On success, we slowly increase toward the configured max (AIMD-like behavior) to recover throughput without oscillation. We also respect `Retry-After` when present and otherwise default to exponential backoff using the exporter retry policy. Checkpointing/dedup: We do not attempt exactly-once delivery. Each log item includes `event_id` as `redwood.event_id` (tag + attribute) so customers can dedup. We preserve ordering within a partition best-effort but do not guarantee strict order under retries. Security/PII: The adapter redacts sensitive fields in logs and avoids logging payload contents. Exporter logs include only: destination id, batch size, response status, dd request id (when provided), and retry wait. API key is loaded via secret ref; no key material in config logs. Tests: Adds unit tests for tag mapping (category->tag), and integration-ish tests via stubbed intake server covering 200, 429 with Retry-After, and 503 transient errors. Operators: Adds additional metrics for adapter status codes and rate-limit backoff behavior to help SRE tune defaults. Rollout: Feature-flagged behind destination validation at control plane; exporter supports the destination type when present in config sync. Follow-ups: (1) support additional Datadog intake paths for Gov/US3 sites (config already allows override); (2) add customer-facing docs and a Console “Test connection” for Datadog (tracked elsewhere).

title: Routing: enforce residency region pinning + disable cross-region fallback by default
preview:
Summary: Integrates Hosted Data Residency “region pinning” policy evaluation into Smart Routing decisions. Requests are constrained to the policy’s allowed_regions and will no longer fail over cross-region by default. Adds structured decision context to routing logs/spans for traceability and downstream error shaping.

Why: We’ve had customer incidents where routing attempted cross-region fallback when a customer expected EU-only processing (see SUP-28441). This PR ensures routing respects residency constraints deterministically, and makes the decision observable (selected region, policy source, and block reason).

Behavior changes: 1) Policy evaluation: Router calls ResidencyPolicyService (cached) at request admission to resolve the effective residency policy (API key > project > org precedence). 2) Region selection: Candidate regions list is intersected with policy.allowed_regions. If the intersection is empty, the request is blocked at routing with a typed error (ResViolated) including a reason code; the gateway PR will map this to the public error contract. 3) Fallback: Cross-region fallback is disabled by default when a residency policy is present. If policy.allow_cross_region_failover=true (explicit) then fallback may occur but only within policy.allowed_regions. 4) Dedicated alignment: Dedicated routes already run in a customer-selected region; for shared routing codepaths we still apply the same allowed_regions intersection to avoid accidental spillover.

Observability: Adds a structured “residency_decision” object to router logs and tracing span attributes (no customer content). Fields include policy_source (org/project/api_key), policy_id (internal UUID), allowed_regions (internal region IDs), primary_region, selected_region, fallback_attempted, fallback_blocked, decision (allow|block), block_reason, and eval_latency_ms.

Safety/rollout: Feature-gated behind routing.residency_enforcement.enabled (default OFF). Dogfood plan is to enable in staging first (INT-9312) and then progressive production rollout by org allowlist.

Testing: Adds unit tests for policy precedence, candidate region intersection, and fallback behavior. Adds a routing integration test harness with synthetic policy responses and verifies that the selected region is stable across retries and that cross-region fallback is blocked.

Notes: This PR intentionally does NOT implement the public HTTP error payload. That is handled in pr-21533 (gateway). This PR focuses on routing correctness + decision tracing.
text:
Summary: Integrates Hosted Data Residency “region pinning” policy evaluation into Smart Routing decisions. Requests are constrained to the policy’s allowed_regions and will no longer fail over cross-region by default. Adds structured decision context to routing logs/spans for traceability and downstream error shaping.

Why: We’ve had customer incidents where routing attempted cross-region fallback when a customer expected EU-only processing (see SUP-28441). This PR ensures routing respects residency constraints deterministically, and makes the decision observable (selected region, policy source, and block reason).

Behavior changes: 1) Policy evaluation: Router calls ResidencyPolicyService (cached) at request admission to resolve the effective residency policy (API key > project > org precedence). 2) Region selection: Candidate regions list is intersected with policy.allowed_regions. If the intersection is empty, the request is blocked at routing with a typed error (ResViolated) including a reason code; the gateway PR will map this to the public error contract. 3) Fallback: Cross-region fallback is disabled by default when a residency policy is present. If policy.allow_cross_region_failover=true (explicit) then fallback may occur but only within policy.allowed_regions. 4) Dedicated alignment: Dedicated routes already run in a customer-selected region; for shared routing codepaths we still apply the same allowed_regions intersection to avoid accidental spillover.

Observability: Adds a structured “residency_decision” object to router logs and tracing span attributes (no customer content). Fields include policy_source (org/project/api_key), policy_id (internal UUID), allowed_regions (internal region IDs), primary_region, selected_region, fallback_attempted, fallback_blocked, decision (allow|block), block_reason, and eval_latency_ms.

Safety/rollout: Feature-gated behind routing.residency_enforcement.enabled (default OFF). Dogfood plan is to enable in staging first (INT-9312) and then progressive production rollout by org allowlist.

Testing: Adds unit tests for policy precedence, candidate region intersection, and fallback behavior. Adds a routing integration test harness with synthetic policy responses and verifies that the selected region is stable across retries and that cross-region fallback is blocked.

Notes: This PR intentionally does NOT implement the public HTTP error payload. That is handled in pr-21533 (gateway). This PR focuses on routing correctness + decision tracing.

title: Hotfix: close authz bypass on streaming chat route (deny-by-default + regression test)
preview:
Incident: See Slack #incidents thread “authz regression on streaming endpoint” (sources/slack/incidents/1740151207-authz-regression-on-streaming-endpoint.json). During RBAC v2 rollout, we discovered that one streaming code path could start an SSE response without having a permission check applied. This was not an intentional allowlist; it was a routing/middleware gap that effectively bypassed deny-by-default for a specific handler. Impact: unauthorized callers could receive streamed tokens on the affected route if they had valid authentication but lacked the required permission; audit logs still captured request metadata but did not record an authz decision for these responses because evaluation never ran.

Root cause: The streaming handler for chat completions registers under a separate router group (legacy streaming mux) which did not have the gateway authz middleware attached. Non-streaming requests hit the standard stack and were correctly blocked. Streaming requests (Accept: text/event-stream or stream=true) were routed to the legacy mux, which performed authentication but skipped the permission enforcement hook.

Fix: (1) Attach the RBAC v2 permission middleware to the legacy streaming mux/router group to ensure deny-by-default applies consistently. (2) Add an explicit preflight authz check in the streaming handler before writing headers/starting the event stream, to prevent partial responses if middleware ordering is changed in the future. (3) Ensure we emit an authz decision audit record for streaming denies (no bytes written) so that evidence remains complete.

Required permission: redwood.inference.chat.completions.stream (existing permission name aligned with ADR-014 naming conventions; no new permission added in this hotfix to keep scope minimal).

Regression tests: Added an integration test that calls the streaming endpoint with a token missing the streaming permission and asserts: HTTP 403, no SSE frames, and denial reason code is set (admin debug header only when enabled). The test also covers the “headers already sent” edge by verifying the authz check runs before any write/flush.

Rollout: This is a hotfix intended for immediate merge and deployment to Hosted first, followed by Dedicated/Private release branches via normal backport process. No customer-visible behavior change for authorized callers; unauthorized callers now receive consistent 403 responses.

Notes: This PR is intentionally small and does not change role templates or permission inventory. Follow-ups for expanding coverage gates across all streaming routes are tracked in ENG-18492.

title: Filtering engine v1: allowlist/denylist by category/action/resource
preview: Implements filtering engine v1 for SIEM-ready audit export (ENG-5160). This adds a small, explicit rule model (allowlist/denylist) that can be evaluated per-event in the exporter with deterministic semantics and safe defaults. Scope in this PR: (1) filter model + evaluation logic (categories/actions/resource predicates), (2) strict config validation + normalization (including “deny wins” and invalid rule rejection), and (3) comprehensive unit tests for tricky cases (empty filters, conflicting allow/deny, unknown categories, wildcard handling, resource/action normalization, timestamp boundary behavior). Motivation: we need customer-configurable filtering that is auditable, easy to reason about during incident response, and stable enough to map to UI + control-plane API without frequent behavior changes. Semantics (as implemented): The effective policy is evaluated in this order: validate -> normalize -> evaluate per event. If filters are absent/null, exporter passes all events (current beta default). If allowlist rules exist, an event must match at least one allow rule to be included. Deny rules always override allow rules (i.e., if an event matches any deny rule, it is excluded even if it also matches allow). Matching: category/action are exact match against canonical strings; resource matching supports resource.type exact match, and resource.id supports either exact match or a simple prefix pattern of the form "prefix:*" (to support common shapes like "workspace:*" without introducing a full glob/regex surface). If a rule specifies multiple fields, the rule matches only if all specified fields match (AND). If a filter list contains unknown categories/actions (not in the exporter’s current taxonomy allowlist), config is rejected and exporter will not start that tenant’s pipeline (surfaced as config error + status). This is intentional for beta to avoid silently dropping/including unexpected events; we can revisit “unknown = no match” for GA if we need forward-compat behavior. Notes: This PR is aligned with ADR-audit-export-filtering-model (allow/deny + deterministic evaluation) and keeps the API surface minimal. The control plane is expected to perform the same validation; exporter validation is defense-in-depth in case of stale/buggy configs. Follow-ups: (a) wire up exporter status endpoint to include “filter_validation_error” details (truncated/safe) for Console, (b) add microbench for high-volume match evaluation, (c) consider forward-compat for new categories (feature-flagged behavior) once schema v2 taxonomy is stable. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for matching + precedence - [x] Validation errors are structured and redact sensitive fields - [x] Metrics: counter for filtered_out_total by tenant + category (bounded cardinality) - [x] Docs snippet added to internal README for config examples - [ ] E2E coverage will land in ENG-5195 (separate PR)

title: Add Splunk HEC destination adapter (gzip compression, 429 backoff, per-token circuit breaker)
preview: Summary: Implements a new Splunk HEC destination adapter for SIEM-ready audit exports. The adapter supports gzip-compressed payloads, robust handling of downstream throttling (429) with Retry-After-aware exponential backoff + jitter, and a per-token circuit breaker to avoid hot-looping on bad credentials or persistent throttling. Motivation: Beta customers are primarily Splunk-first and we observed exporter lag spikes when HEC returns sustained 429s; this change improves delivery stability and reduces noisy retry behavior. Scope: Adapter implementation + config schema + basic unit tests + metrics hooks. Not included: Splunk indexer acknowledgment (HEC ACK) support (will track separately), advanced event field remapping beyond the audit schema’s SIEM mapping guidance. Delivery semantics: remains at-least-once; downstream Splunk consumers should dedupe on event_id if needed. Config: adds splunk_hec destination type with endpoint_url, token_ref, index/source/sourcetype (optional), max_batch_events/max_batch_bytes, flush_interval, request_timeout, max_retries, circuit_breaker thresholds and cooldown. Security: token is read via secret ref only; adapter logs redact token and never log full request bodies; destination validation ensures https and blocks loopback/private ranges when egress allowlist is enabled. Observability: adds adapter-level counters for requests, bytes, response codes, retries, breaker_open, and last_success_timestamp; integrates into existing exporter metrics namespace. Test plan: unit tests for batching, gzip encoding, retry/backoff timing (using fake clock), circuit breaker transitions; local manual validation against Splunk HEC sandbox container with artificial 429 injection. Checklist: [x] unit tests added/updated [x] lint/format [x] docs updated for destination config [x] metrics added [ ] e2e harness coverage (follow-up in ENG-5195).

title: Add dead-letter queue + guarded replay CLI for failed export batches
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for audit export batches that fail delivery after exhausting retries, and adds an operator-facing replay tool with guardrails. This is a reliability/safety feature for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (Splunk HEC / Datadog Logs / S3) to ensure we never silently drop events while still protecting the platform and customers from accidental replay storms. Context / Motivation: During downstream outages (e.g., sustained 429s from Splunk HEC or 403s from mis-scoped Datadog keys), the exporter currently retries with backoff and then surfaces a per-destination error. Without DLQ persistence we risk either (a) losing batches on process/queue churn or (b) having no safe operational path to replay specific failures once a customer fixes credentials/quotas. ENG-5164 calls for a DLQ + replay tooling with explicit operator controls. Design highlights: (1) DLQ record model: When a batch hits max_delivery_attempts OR we detect a non-retryable adapter error, we persist a DLQ record keyed by (tenant_id, export_config_id, checkpoint_range) and store the batch payload (newline-delimited JSON events) in object storage under a structured prefix. DLQ record includes: dlq_id, created_at, tenant_id, export_config_id, destination_type, reason_code, reason_detail (redacted), first_event_ts, last_event_ts, event_count, byte_size, last_attempt_at, attempt_count, and a payload_ref. (2) At-least-once compatibility: Replays may cause duplicate deliveries. We intentionally do not attempt exactly-once; downstream should dedup using event_id (per ADR on delivery semantics). Replay CLI logs a reminder and requires an explicit --ack-duplicates flag. (3) Operator replay CLI: Adds bin/audit-exporter-replay (Go) to list DLQ items and replay in a controlled fashion. Guardrails include: required --tenant-id and --export-config-id; default is dry-run; interactive confirmation unless --yes is provided; bounded selection via --since/--until and --max-items; token-bucket rate limiting (--max-events-per-minute) to avoid spiking adapters; refuses to replay if export config is disabled OR if exporter feature flag is off for that tenant (unless --override-feature-flag is provided, which is restricted to on-call breakglass role). (4) Safety checks: Replay validates destination configuration hash matches the DLQ record by default (prevents replaying old payloads into a newly re-pointed sink). Can be overridden with --ignore-config-hash-mismatch for incident response, but emits a prominent warning. (5) Redaction: DLQ reason_detail is run through the same redaction helper used by adapter logs to avoid accidentally persisting tokens/keys/PII. Payloads contain audit events only (already subject to audit schema/PII policy); we do not include request/response bodies. (6) Observability: Adds metrics: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total{tenant_id_hash,destination_type,reason_code}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_items{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog_events{destination_type}, audit_exporter_dlq_replay_total{result}, and audit_exporter_dlq_replay_latency_seconds. Also adds structured logs for DLQ enqueue and replay runs (with correlation_id). User-visible / Ops workflow: - When an adapter can’t deliver a batch and we exceed retry limits, exporter transitions the batch to DLQ and advances the live pipeline checkpoint to prevent system-wide backpressure. - On-call or Support can instruct customer to fix auth/limits, then run replay for the impacted tenant/config/time window. - Replay is incremental and rate-limited; any failures during replay are re-DLQ’d with a new reason_code (replay_failed_*). Example commands: - List backlog: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq list --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --until 2025-02-09T06:00:00Z - Dry-run replay (default): ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 10 - Execute replay with explicit acknowledgements: ./bin/audit-exporter-replay dlq replay --tenant-id tnt_123 --export-config-id aex_456 --since 2025-02-09T00:00:00Z --max-items 50 --max-events-per-minute 12000 --ack-duplicates --yes Testing: - Unit tests for DLQ persistence, reason_code mapping, and redaction - Integration tests using adapter stubs: force 429/403/non-retryable to verify DLQ enqueue and payload roundtrip - Manual: replayed a synthetic backlog into Splunk stub with rate limiting and verified dedup by event_id Related: - Linear: ENG-5164 - ADR: delivery semantics and idempotency (at-least-once + event_id) - Support context: SUP-18531 (perceived missing events during throttling) Notes / Follow-ups: - Dashboard panels/alerts for DLQ backlog are tracked separately (we add metrics here, panels in observability-pack). - For beta, replay CLI is intended for on-call / SRE / Support escalation only; we are not exposing self-serve replay in Console.

title: Support backup-oriented batch export mode (pagination + retention guardrails)
preview:
Context: Private DR / compliance asks require a repeatable, non-interactive way to export audit logs for backup/retention evidence. The existing exporter is optimized for ad-hoc, interactive exports and can be slow/fragile when used in scheduled jobs (offset-based pagination, no checkpointing, limited safeguards around retention boundaries).

This PR adds a backup-oriented batch export mode designed for scheduled backups:
- New CLI subcommand: `audit-log-exporter export batch`
- Cursor-based pagination using the API’s stable `after_id`/`next_cursor` semantics (no offset)
- Checkpointing/resume: writes a checkpoint token to disk periodically; supports `--resume` to continue without duplicating output
- Retention guardrails: validates requested time window against server-reported retention (if available) and fails fast with actionable messaging; explicit override flag required to proceed when outside retention
- Bounded exports: `--max-pages`, `--page-size`, and `--max-records` to prevent runaway jobs
- Deterministic output layout suitable for backup:
  - JSONL lines (one event per line)
  - Rotates files by `--chunk-records` or `--chunk-bytes`
  - Writes a manifest containing record counts, min/max event timestamps, and sha256 checksums
- Backoff + retry tuning for long-running jobs, with explicit handling for rate limits and transient gateway errors

Notes:
- This intentionally does not encrypt output itself; at-rest encryption remains the responsibility of the storage backend (S3 SSE-KMS / GCS CMEK / NFS-at-rest). The docs and examples call this out and link to Private backup storage recommendations.
- The exporter continues to support the existing `export` path unchanged.

Testing:
- Unit tests for pagination correctness (no gaps/dupes), checkpoint/resume behavior, and manifest generation
- Integration-ish test using a stub server to emulate retention responses + cursor pagination

Follow-ups:
- Add Prometheus metrics when we wire this into the Private scheduled backup CronJob path (tracked in ENG-9826 for the broader toolkit metrics/alerts).

title: Emit metrics + tracing for Private backup/restore operations (with error categorization)
preview: Motivation: SRE/on-call and customers need a consistent way to answer “are backups running?” and “why did the last restore drill fail?” across VPC + on-prem Private installs. We already log backup/restore steps in the installer, but logs alone are hard to alert on and don’t support reliable aggregation. This PR adds: (1) structured Prometheus metrics for backup + restore operations (success/failure, duration, bytes, last success timestamps), (2) OpenTelemetry tracing spans with consistent attributes for per-step visibility, and (3) explicit error categorization to drive dashboards/alerts and reduce pager noise. Scope: This instruments the control-plane backup/restore path (installer-driven + control-plane endpoints) and adds common helpers used by both “backup create” and “restore apply”. It does not change backup artifact format or encryption behavior. Metrics (Prometheus): - redwood_private_backup_runs_total{result,storage_backend,component,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_duration_seconds_bucket{result,storage_backend,component} (histogram) - redwood_private_backup_bytes_total{result,storage_backend,component} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds{storage_backend,component,format_version} (gauge) - redwood_private_restore_runs_total{result,source_backend,target_env,format_version} (counter) - redwood_private_restore_duration_seconds_bucket{result,step} (histogram) - redwood_private_restore_validation_failures_total{check_name,error_category} (counter) - redwood_private_backup_restore_errors_total{operation,step,error_category} (counter; emitted on failure paths only) Tracing (OTel): Adds a top-level span per operation: “private.backup.create” and “private.restore.apply”, with child spans for steps: preflight, quiesce, snapshot, encrypt, upload, verify, bootstrap, migrate, validate. Spans include attributes: deployment_id, cluster_id (if known), namespace, storage_backend, artifact_manifest_version, backup_id/restore_id, step, result, error_category. Error categorization: Introduces a shared enum + mapping helper that classifies common failure types in a stable way for alerting/reporting. Categories include: preflight_failed, quiesce_failed, snapshot_failed, encrypt_failed, upload_failed, download_failed, verify_failed, bootstrap_failed, migrate_failed, validation_failed, compatibility_failed, permission_denied, kms_unavailable, timeout, rate_limited, unknown. Mapping is conservative: if we can’t confidently classify, we emit unknown and preserve the raw error message in logs/traces (not as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality explosions. RBAC / permissions: No new privileges are required for the control plane, but installer pods/jobs must be allowed to emit metrics to the existing collector in Private installs. This PR reuses existing OTEL exporter wiring and the in-cluster Prometheus scrape config. Testing: - Unit tests for error classification mapping and label sanitization - Integration test in staging that runs a forced failure (bad bucket creds) and asserts metrics increments + spans have error_category - Manual validation: ran backup create / restore apply in dev VPC env; verified dashboard panels populate (see follow-up in observability-pack PR). Notes on cardinality: - No metric labels include backup_id, restore_id, customer name, or raw error strings - deployment_id is included only as a tracing attribute (not a metric label) - component label is constrained to a small set (control_plane_db, control_plane_config, k8s_manifests, audit_logs_export) - step label is constrained to known step names and used only on histogram for restore duration per step (bounded) Follow-ups: - Alert rules + dashboards land in observability-pack (PR-94) - SLO definitions tracked in slo-toolkit (PR-67) - Backup status endpoint is in PR-24318 (separate) which will consume these metrics for UI surfacing

title: Private: expose backup status endpoints from control plane
preview:
Motivation: Private customers (and our own SRE/Solutions workflows) need a consistent way to surface “backup readiness” without scraping CronJob logs or inferring state from storage backends. This PR adds control-plane API endpoints that report backup status and last-success timestamps per tenant/installation, with strict authz and safe redaction (no bucket names, object keys, or secret material). This unblocks Console UI + automated restore drills to gate on “recent successful backup” and to page on backup job regressions.

What’s included: Adds a BackupStatus read model persisted by the backup orchestrator after each run (success/failure), keyed by tenant_id and environment_id; Read-only endpoints under the Private API surface; Includes timestamps (last_success_at, last_attempt_at), status enum, last_error_category + last_error_message (truncated), artifact metadata (format_version, bytes_total), and a computed “staleness” field based on configured schedule; RBAC scopes: tenant:read plus a new private:backup_status:read for installer/service automation; Optional cluster-wide endpoint for platform operators (requires internal operator role); Adds OpenAPI spec + SDK client stub used by Console; Adds audit log events for reads of backup status (low volume) and writes on backup completion.

Endpoints: GET /v1/private/tenants/{tenant_id}/backup/status -> returns latest status record for that tenant; GET /v1/private/backup/status -> operator-only list endpoint with pagination/filtering by status/staleness (intended for internal fleet dashboards; not exposed in customer docs yet).

Non-goals / out of scope: No “trigger backup” endpoint (installer/cronjob remains source of truth); No exposure of artifact locations (bucket/key) or encryption/key IDs; No attempt to infer state by reading object storage; This does not change backup/restore format.

Security + privacy notes: last_error_message is truncated to 512 chars and scrubbed via existing log redaction utility; artifact_metadata excludes backend identifiers; list endpoint is operator-only and excluded from external docs generation.

Testing: Unit tests for status model serialization, authz gates, and redaction; API integration tests covering success and failure flows; Added a contract test asserting the response schema remains backwards compatible (fields only added, never removed) to support Console; Load test sanity: list endpoint paginates and enforces max page size.

Rollout plan: Ship behind feature flag private.backupStatusEndpoints (default on for staging, off for prod) with a follow-up to enable for Private GA once Console is ready; SRE to validate via restore drill automation and dashboard wiring.

Checklist: [x] OpenAPI updated; [x] Authz reviewed (Marcus); [x] Audit logging for reads/writes; [x] Metrics emitted on write path (status_write_success, status_write_latency_ms); [x] Docs note added to internal runbook; [x] Backwards compatible response contract.

Refs: Linear ENG-9826/ENG-9827; related installer work in redwood-private-installer PR-482/PR-489; observability pack PR-94 depends on status fields last_success_at + staleness_minutes.

title: gateway: add RBAC v2 authz middleware (deny-by-default) + standardized 403s
control-plane: add residency policy schema + validation + admin endpoints (with migrations)
preview: Summary: Introduces first-class residency policies in the control plane, including schema + persistence, validation rules, admin CRUD endpoints, and a migration path for existing orgs/projects. This is the foundational layer used by routing/gateway enforcement (separate PRs) and by Console settings UX. Key points: (1) New entity: ResidencyPolicy with scope=org|project|api_key, allowed_regions[] + primary_region, and explicit fallback controls. (2) Validation: deterministic, fail-closed on invalid configs; prevents primary_region not in allowed set; blocks ambiguous overlaps at same scope; enforces precedence rules (api_key > project > org) while allowing inherit semantics. (3) Admin API: internal-only endpoints behind RBAC permission control_plane.residency_policy:read|write; supports list/get/create/update/delete and dry-run validate. (4) Migration: seeds default “inherit/unrestricted” policies for existing orgs (no behavior change) and adds nullable columns/tables behind a feature flag for staged rollout. (5) Tests: unit tests for validation matrix + precedence + migration; API contract tests for admin endpoints. Notes: This PR intentionally does NOT change routing behavior; it only provides config + decision inputs for downstream enforcement.

title: RBAC v2: add permission enum + registry (metadata + CI validation)
preview: Summary: Introduces a canonical permission registry for RBAC v2 in the redwood monorepo. This adds a typed Permission enum (backed by stable string IDs), a registry with required metadata (description, owning team, sensitivity tier, allowed scopes), and CI validation to enforce uniqueness + naming conventions. Motivation: We need a single source of truth for "what permissions exist" before we can (a) annotate endpoints/jobs consistently, (b) generate role templates/policy bundles, and (c) provide audit/evidence artifacts for enterprise customers. This PR intentionally includes only an initial catalog covering the first critical workflows (org/user/role management, API key management, audit log export, routing policy edits) so downstream adoption work can proceed in parallel. Design notes: Permission IDs follow ADR-014 conventions (namespaced dot-separated strings, no implicit wildcards, no environment-specific IDs; scoping is handled in bindings). Metadata is intentionally strict and required for every permission to avoid future "mystery permissions". The registry is compile-time accessible for services and is also exportable as JSON for docs/Console. Implementation details: Added rbac/permissions/Permission.ts (enum), rbac/permissions/registry.ts (PermissionDefinition map), a registry export command (prints machine-readable catalog), and a CI linter step (permissions-lint) that checks: (1) unique IDs, (2) regex match for allowed format, (3) required metadata fields present, (4) no duplicate descriptions with different IDs, (5) deprecated permissions must include replacement + sunset date. Also added unit tests around the linter and registry export. Follow-ups: Endpoint annotations (ENG-18305) will reference Permission enum values directly; Gateway middleware (ENG-18277) will use the registry to populate denial reason context; Docs (redwood-docs PR) will consume the exported catalog. Checklist: [x] Permission ID naming matches ADR-014; [x] Initial inventory reviewed w/ Security Eng; [x] CI enforcement added; [x] Export format documented; [ ] Backfill remaining permissions (tracked in ENG-18241). Risk: Low. This is additive and not yet wired into enforcement paths. CI: New step added to buildkite pipeline; should run <2s.

title: Add differential prioritizer + regression demux CI hook for stratified smoke sampling
preview:
Context and motivation: We continue to see noisy regression alerts from broad smoke-suites where rare but high-variance prompts swamp triage cycles. This PR introduces a sampling prioritizer that differentially weights prompts by historical variance, recent degradation signal, and business impact metadata, and a CI hook (regression-demux) that demultiplexes failing prompts into per-model/cohort lanes for targeted replays and quarantines. The goal is to reduce alert fatigue while preserving early detection for high-impact regressions.

What this PR does (high level): implements eval_harness.samplers.differential_prioritizer, extends sample metadata to carry priority scores and buckets, adds a ci/hooks/regression_demux adapter that: (1) inspects CI eval failures, (2) enriches failures with priority and cohort context, (3) routes items to dedicated replay queues and labeling workflows, and (4) triggers adaptive backoff (soft-fail) to avoid alert storms. Also wires telemetry tags and adds a lightweight dashboard event for triage.

Design notes: prioritizer computes a continuous priority score = alpha*normalized_variance + beta*recent_failure_rate + gamma*business_impact where coefficients are runtime-configurable via eval_harness/config/prioritizer.yaml. Stratification is performed by prompt-cohort (semantic fingerprint bucket), model-variant, and customer-tier. The demux CI hook receives the flattened eval failure payload and atomically annotates each sample with: priority_score, cohort_id, last_seen_ts, and deterministic_replay_key. Deterministic_replay_key allows idempotent replay deduping across retries and workers. The hook integrates with existing replay queues (replay-q/*) and labels failing prompts for downstream annotators.

Backward compatibility: All changes are internal to the harness. The external eval API and prompt-set definitions are unchanged. The prioritizer is off by default and gated behind a config flag (eval.harness.prioritizer.enabled=false). The demux hook is installed as opt-in CI stage: ci.stages: [smoke, demux].

Testing: unit tests for prioritizer scoring and bucket assignment; integration test that simulates a burst of targeted failures and asserts that top-k priority prompts are routed to high-priority replay lanes; end-to-end smoke in CI with a seeded evaluation dataset to validate deterministic_replay_key behavior. Added a small synthetic benchmark to ensure prioritizer scoring adds <2ms/sample overhead on typical eval batches.

Rollback plan: toggle eval.harness.prioritizer.enabled=false and remove the demux stage from CI config. Replay queues are append-only and safe to ignore if demux is disabled. If we observe disproportionate false positives, we will revert the deployment and backfill audit logs for the window.

Risks and mitigations: risk of mis-calibrated weights causing low-impact prompts to be surfaced — mitigated by conservative default coefficients and a 2-week canary with A/B split. Risk of increased CI runtime due to tagging — mitigated by asynchronous enqueueing for low-priority samples and batch writes for telemetry tags.

Checklist:
- [x] Prioritizer implementation + unit tests
- [x] CI hook implementation + integration tests
- [x] Telemetry tagging and dashboard event
- [x] Feature flag and config file
- [x] Documentation snippet in docs/ci-hooks.md

Related: ENG-4123 (sampling roadmap), ENG-4200 (ci alert-rate project).
